title: Concussions
summary: Recognize the signs of a concussion, respond correctly on the sideline, and understand why 'when in doubt, sit them out' is the law.

sections.heading: What a concussion is
body: A concussion is a mild traumatic brain injury caused by a bump, blow, or jolt to the head — or to the body — that makes the brain move rapidly inside the skull. The sudden movement stretches brain cells and triggers chemical changes that temporarily disrupt normal brain function.

Two things surprise most students: a concussion does not require a direct hit to the head (a hard body check can do it), and loss of consciousness happens in fewer than 10% of concussions. Most concussed athletes never black out.

sections.heading: Signs and symptoms
body: Signs are what you observe; symptoms are what the athlete reports. Symptoms fall into four categories:
list: Physical — headache (most common symptom), dizziness, nausea or vomiting, balance problems, sensitivity to light or noise, blurry vision.
Cognitive — feeling foggy or slowed down, confusion, trouble concentrating or remembering, answering questions slowly, repeating questions.
Emotional — irritability, sadness, nervousness, being 'not themselves.'
Sleep — drowsiness, sleeping more or less than usual (relevant in the days after).

sections.heading: Observable signs on the field
body: Watch for: a dazed or stunned look, forgetting plays or assignments, confusion about score or opponent, moving clumsily, answering slowly, personality change, and any loss of consciousness — even seconds.
Memory questions (Maddocks questions) help reveal confusion: 'What venue are we at?' 'Which half is it?' 'Who scored last?' Wrong answers to questions like these are a red flag for concussion.

sections.heading: Sideline response
body: If a concussion is suspected: remove the athlete from play immediately. They do not return that day — no exceptions, no 'shaking it off.' Every U.S. state has a law requiring removal and written clearance by a licensed healthcare provider before return.
Never leave a possibly concussed athlete alone; symptoms can worsen over the first hours. Notify the athletic trainer, coach, and parents. Do not let them drive.
Students never make the return-to-play call. Your job is recognition and removal, then handing off to the athletic trainer or physician.

callout.title: Emergency red flags — call 911
callout.text: One pupil larger than the other, worsening or severe headache, repeated vomiting, seizure, slurred speech, increasing confusion or agitation, weakness or numbness, unusual drowsiness or inability to wake — these suggest a more serious brain injury (such as a bleed) and are a medical emergency. Suspected neck injury? Do not move the athlete; stabilize the head and call 911.

sections.heading: Second impact syndrome
body: If an athlete takes a second head impact before the first concussion has healed, the brain can lose its ability to regulate blood flow, causing rapid, often fatal brain swelling. This is called second impact syndrome, and adolescents are the highest-risk group.
This is why 'when in doubt, sit them out' is not cautious advice — it is the entire point of concussion protocols.

sections.heading: Recovery and return to play
body: Most concussions resolve in 1–4 weeks with adolescents typically taking longer than adults. Recovery starts with 24–48 hours of relative rest (light activity is fine; total dark-room isolation is outdated), followed by a gradual return to school first, then sport.

Return to play follows a stepwise progression, each stage at least 24 hours apart: light aerobic exercise → sport-specific exercise → non-contact drills → full-contact practice → competition. Any symptom return means dropping back a stage. Written medical clearance is required before contact.

callout.title: Baseline testing
callout.text: Many programs test athletes' memory, reaction time, and balance before the season (e.g., ImPACT testing). Comparing post-injury scores to the athlete's own baseline helps clinicians judge recovery — another reason honest effort on baseline tests matters.

quiz.question: A soccer player collides with an opponent, never loses consciousness, but seems dazed and asks 'what's the score?' twice. What should happen?
choices: She sits out the rest of the day and is referred for evaluation
She can return after 15 symptom-free minutes on the bench
She can return if she passes a sprint test
Nothing — no loss of consciousness means no concussion
quiz.explanation: Repeating questions and appearing dazed are concussion signs. A suspected concussion means immediate removal with no same-day return — and fewer than 10% of concussions involve loss of consciousness.

quiz.question: What is the most commonly reported concussion symptom?
choices: Headache
Vomiting
Loss of consciousness
Neck pain
quiz.explanation: Headache is the most common symptom. Loss of consciousness is actually uncommon — under 10% of cases.

quiz.question: Which finding after a head impact means calling 911, not just sideline monitoring?
choices: One pupil larger than the other
A mild headache
Sensitivity to bright light
Feeling 'foggy'
quiz.explanation: Unequal pupils suggest pressure on the brain, such as bleeding — an emergency. Mild headache, light sensitivity, and fogginess are typical concussion symptoms that still require removal, but not necessarily 911.

quiz.question: Why is second impact syndrome the reason for strict no-same-day-return rules?
choices: A second impact before healing can cause rapid, often fatal brain swelling
A second impact always breaks the skull
It doubles the length of the athlete's suspension
It only affects professional athletes
quiz.explanation: A second blow before the first concussion heals can disrupt the brain's blood-flow regulation, causing catastrophic swelling. Adolescents are at highest risk.

quiz.question: Can an athlete get a concussion without being hit in the head?
choices: Yes — a hard blow to the body can whip the brain inside the skull
No — direct head contact is required
Only if they also break a bone
Only in collision sports like football
quiz.explanation: Any force that jolts the head — including a hard body hit — can make the brain move rapidly inside the skull and cause a concussion.

quiz.question: An athlete is cleared to begin the return-to-play progression. During non-contact drills her headache returns. What's the correct move?
choices: Drop back a stage and progress again once symptom-free
Push through — symptoms during exercise are expected
Skip ahead to full contact to test her tolerance
End her season immediately
quiz.explanation: The stepwise progression (each stage ≥24 hours) requires dropping back a stage whenever symptoms return, then advancing again once symptom-free.

quiz.question: Who can clear a high school athlete to return to contact after a concussion?
choices: A licensed healthcare provider, in writing
The head coach, verbally
The team captain
The athlete, once they feel fine
quiz.explanation: State law requires written clearance from a licensed healthcare provider. Coaches, students, and the athlete themselves cannot make that call.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of preseason baseline testing like ImPACT?
choices: It gives clinicians the athlete's own normal scores to compare against after an injury
It predicts which athletes will get concussed
It replaces the need for medical clearance
It measures physical strength
quiz.explanation: Baseline tests record the athlete's normal memory, reaction time, and balance so post-injury results can be compared to their own baseline rather than a population average.

flashcards.back: A mild traumatic brain injury from a bump, blow, or jolt to the head or body that makes the brain move rapidly in the skull, temporarily disrupting brain function.

flashcards.front: How often does loss of consciousness occur with concussion?
flashcards.back: Fewer than 10% of concussions — most concussed athletes never black out.
Physical (headache, dizziness), cognitive (foggy, confused), emotional (irritable, 'not themselves'), sleep (more or less than usual).
Memory questions like 'What venue is this? Which half is it? Who scored last?' — wrong answers signal concussion-related confusion.
Remove from play immediately, no same-day return, never leave them alone, refer to a licensed provider. Students never make return-to-play decisions.
Unequal pupils, worsening headache, repeated vomiting, seizure, slurred speech, increasing confusion, weakness/numbness, can't be awakened.
A second head impact before the first concussion heals can cause rapid, often fatal brain swelling. Adolescents are highest risk — the reason for 'when in doubt, sit them out.'
Light aerobic → sport-specific → non-contact drills → full contact → competition. Each stage ≥24 hours; symptoms returning = drop back a stage. Written clearance required.
Preseason measurement of memory, reaction time, and balance (e.g., ImPACT) so post-injury scores can be compared to the athlete's own normal.
